[كَانَ (قَدْ) قَتَلَ]{.ar}
can be used in the C to indicate an event which has already occurred.
In such cases,
the CR will typically be introduced by [فَ]{.ar}.
For example:
-->

<!--
#### The CR-core signifies a event which is expected to definitely occur {.unnumbered}

[فَ]{.ar} can be used to introduce a verbal sentence CR.
to make it more independent from the C,
and to signify that the event indicated by the CR is expected to definitely occur
(if the C is met).
-->

#### The CR-verb is preceded by a particle other than [لَمْ]{.ar}

When the CR-verb is preceded by [لَمْ]{.ar}
then it typically occurs directly in the CR without being introduced by [فَ]{.ar}.
But when the CR-verb is preceded by another particle
like [سَ]{.ar}, [سَوْفَ]{.ar}, or [لَنْ]{.ar},
then a [فَ]{.ar} is required.
For example:

(@) [إِنْ كُنْتِ بَرِيئَةً **فَسَيُبَرِّئُكِ** اللَّهُ وَإِنْ كُنْتِ أَلْمَمْتِ بِذَنْبٍ فَاسْتَغْفِرِي اللَّهَ وَتُوبِي إِلَيْهِ]{.ar}
    [[[صحيح البخاري]{.ar} :4690](https://sunnah.com/bukhari:4690)]{.citex}  

(@) [إِن تَسۡتَغۡفِرۡ لَهُمۡ سَبۡعِینَ مَرَّةࣰ **فَلَن** یَغۡفِرَ ٱللَّهُ لَهُمۡۚ]{.quran}
    [[[سورة التوبة]{.ar} 9:80](https://quran.com/9/80)]{.citex}  
    [If you should ask forgiveness for them seventy times - never will Allāh forgive them.]{.trans}  

Once [فَ]{.ar} introduces the CR,
[مَا]{.ar} then can be used to negate a verb in the CR.
For example:


<!--
Emphasis is on the occurrence (or non-occurrence) of CR.
[فَ]{.ar} is then needed to indicate that even though emphasis is on the occurrence of CR,
it is still linked to C.

#### Summary {.unnumbered}

Generally, [فَ]{.ar} is added to the CR,
when it may not be apparent that CR is linked to C.
So [فَ]{.ar} makes it obvious.
-->

<!--
### [فَ]{.ar} in the CR for [إِذَا]{.ar} conditional sentences
-->

### [إِذَا]{.ar} fujaaiyyah instead of [فَ]{.ar}

### Government of the CR that begins with [فَ]{.ar}

### Omitting the [فَ]{.ar} when it is required

Peled, _Conditional structures in Classical Arabic_ 77--78

### Adding a [فَ]{.ar} when it is not required

### When can [فَ]{.ar} not be used as a CR-intro

When the CR-verb is under the future-shifting influence
or governing effect of the C-intro,
then [فَ]{.ar} may not be used as a CR-intro.
So we cannot say:

(@) ✗ [إِنْ تَأْتِنِي فَآتِكَ]{.ar}  
    (The CR-verb [آتِ]{.ar} is in the ø-case.)


---



The fact that the 
CR is a direct consequence of the C
is shown by:

1. The pattern of the conditional sentence.
1. The matching tense of meanings of the C and the CR.

When the CR is independent of the C,
then a [فَ]{.ar} is typically required to
explicitly indicate that the CR is
a consequence of the C.

---


This is because the patterns of the conditional sentence, that we have studied so far,
implcitly indicate that the CR is a consequence of the C.
These patterns, as we know, are:

1. [إِنْ يَقْتُلْ يَقْتُلْ]{.ar}
1. [إِنْ قَتَلَ قَتَلَ]{.ar}
1. [إِنْ قَتَلَ يَقْتُلْ]{.ar}
1. [إِنْ يَقْتُلْ قَتَلَ]{.ar}

Also included in the above patterns are the negative constructions
[لَا يَقْتُلْ]{.ar}
(in place of [يَقْتُلْ]{.ar})
and
[لَمْ يَقْتُلْ]{.ar}
(in place of [قَتَلَ]{.ar}).

When an [إِنْ]{.ar} conditional sentence falls into one of the above patterns
and 

When the CR falls outside the above patterns,
then a [فَ]{.ar} is typically required to
explicitly indicate that the CR is
a consequence of the C.

---

For example:

In the types of conditional sentences that we have studied so far,
the CR is a verbal sentence that begins

When the CR is a verbal sentence
and the verb in the CR is the direct consequence of the C,
then no intervening [فَ]{.ar} is needed.
Otherwise, a [فَ]{.ar} is usually needed to make it clear that the CR is a consequence of the C.

## The C-intro nouns that govern like [إِنْ]{.ar}

<https://shamela.ws/book/98123/69#p1>:

::: {.ar}
ومن تشبيه "متى" بـ "إذا" وإهمالها قول عائشة رضي الله عنها (إن أبا بكر رجلٌ أسيف، وإنه (١١٨) متى يقومُ مقامك لا يُسمعُ الناس) (١١٩)
:::


## The tense of a conditional sentence REMOVE

FIXME: rework this entire section. See also [هل يأتي الشرط للمضي؟]{.ar} [[معاني النحو]{.ar} 4/64ff]{.citex}

For conditional sentences which have a C-verb or CR-verb,
the tense of either of these two verbs
is determined by context, regardless of whether the verb is in the
[يَقْتُلْ]{.ar} or [قَتَلَ]{.ar} form.

Most contexts will steer the tense to the future.
For example:

in ataytani akramtuka

in ta'tini ukrimka



### The tense and significance of dynamic verbs in a conditional sentence


Dynamic verbs are those that state an event or describe a process,
like [أَتَى]{.ar} [to come]{.trans},
[أَكْرَمَ]{.ar} [to honor]{.trans},
etc.


<!--
So far we have deferred a detailed discussion
of the tense indicated by the C and the CR.
In this section, we will try to do justice to the topic, if Allāh wills.
-->

Earlier we had summarily stated that
the tense signified
in conditional sentences is generally the future tense,
regardless of what form of verb
(for example: ø-case or caseless)
is used.

However, there can be instances of
of a
C-verb
or a
CR-verb
having a past tense.
For example:

(@) [قَالُوۤا۟ إِن **یَسۡرِقۡ** فَقَدۡ سَرَقَ أَخࣱ لَّهُۥ مِن قَبۡلُۚ]{.quran}^[Ibn\ [#mAlik]{.trn2} and [#abU #HayyAn]{.trn2} interpret the C in this [Ayah]{.trn2} as having a past tense.  See [[شواهد التوضيح لابن مالك]{.ar} 231](https://shamela.ws/book/98123/228) and [[البحر المحيط لأبي حيان]{.ar}](https://tafsir.app/albahr-almuheet/12/77) for [سورة يوسف]{.ar} 12:77 respectively.]
    [[[سورة يوسف]{.ar} 12:77](https://quran.com/12/77)]{.citex}  
    [They said, "If he steals - a brother of his has stolen before."]{.trans}

(@) [وَإِذَا **رَأَوۡا۟** تِجَـٰرَةً أَوۡ لَهۡوًا **ٱنفَضُّوۤا۟** إِلَیۡهَا وَتَرَكُوكَ قَاۤىِٕمࣰاۚ]{.quran}
    [[[سورة الجمعة]{.ar} 62:11](https://quran.com/62/11)]{.citex}  
    [But [on one occasion] when they **saw** a transaction or a diversion, [O Muḥammad], they **rushed** to it and left you standing.]{.trans}  

(@) [أَنَّ النَّبِيَّ صلى الله عليه وسلم كَانَ إِذَا أُتِيَ بِطَعَامٍ سَأَلَ عَنْهُ **فَإِنْ قِيلَ** هَدِيَّةٌ **أَكَلَ** مِنْهَا **وَإِنْ قِيلَ** صَدَقَةٌ **لَمْ يَأْكُلْ** مِنْهَا]{.ar}
    [[[صحيح مسلم]{.ar} :1077](https://sunnah.com/muslim:1077)]{.citex}  
    [that the Prophet [ﷺ]{.aralt} would, when he was brought some food, ask about it. If it **was said** [that it was] a gift, he **ate** from it, and if it **was said** [that it was] charity, he **did not eat** from it.]{.trans}  

So how do we relate the forms of the
C-verb
and the CR-verb
to the tense signified by the conditional sentence?
We will attempt to explain this below:

As you know,
the following positive verb forms are used in conditional sentences:

1. The ø-case caseful verb

   (@) [إِنْ **تَأْتِنِي أُكْرِمْكَ**]{.ar}  

2. The caseless verb. For example:

   (@) [إِنْ **أَتَيْتَنِي أَكْرَمْتُكَ**]{.ar}  

   (@) [إِذَا  **أَتَيْتَنِي أَكْرَمْتُكَ**]{.ar}  

3. The u-case caseful verb.

   (@) [إِنْ أَتَيْتَنِي **أُكْرِمُكَ**]{.ar}  

   (@) [إِذَا **تَأْتِينِي أُكْرِمُكَ**]{.ar}  

Not all combinations of the verb forms
are shown in examples () through () above.
Other combinations,
and their relative frequencies of usage,
have been discussed
in sections\ (refs) above.

<!--
The question is,
when is one verb form chosen over another,
and what difference in meaning would it signify?

First, we would like to state
that in many cases the verb forms,
when permissible,
are often interchangeable.
And the nuances in meaning that we will attempt to explain below
are more
connotations and trends
rather than hard and fast rules.
Needless to say, they are also based on our subjective reasoning,
and therefore liable to critique.
-->

We will begin by first listing the essential significances of each of the above verb forms
independent of conditional sentences.
We will then explain how those essential significances can carry over to within conditional sentences.

The essential signification of the
caseless verb form
is that an action
has reached a completed state.
So for example, if we say
[أَتَيْتَنِي]{.ar},
then the caseless verb form signifies that
your action of coming to me has been completed.
The caseless verb has the most certainty of the different verb forms,
because any action that has completed has certainly occurred.

The essential signification of the
u-case caseful verb form
is that
an action
is either on-going,
or that it will occur in the future.
So for example, if we say
[تَأْتِينِي]{.ar},
then the u-case caseful verb form signifies that
your action of coming to me is either on-going,
or that it will occur in the future.

The essential signification of the positive ø-case caseful verb
is that the speaker
considers an action
to be potentially possible.
So for example, if we say
[تَأْتِنِي]{.ar},
then the ø-case caseful verb form indicates
that your coming to me is a potential possibility.^[[التأويل الزمني للجملة الشرطية في اللغة العربية لعارف الدين]{.ar} 49]
<!--
^[[التأويل الزمني للجملة الشرطية في اللغة العربية لعارف الدين ٤٩ (يستشهد أحمد المتوكل، قضايا اللغة العربية في اللسانيات الوظيفية البنية التحتية أو التمثيل الداللي التداولي. الرباط: دار الأمان، ١٩٩٥.)]{.ar}]
-->

The ø-case caseful verb has less certainty compared to the other verb forms
due to its signifying a potential action.

When these verb forms are used in the C
or the CR,
then their essential significations,
as listed above,
can be retained.
But the C-intro allows the tense of the C to be open to all times.

<!--
But the C-intro can allow the scenario of the C,
i.e. the situation in which the C occurs,
to be transported to a different tense.
By default, it will transport the scenario of C to the future,
but it could also be transported to the past.
-->
<!--
The tense of the
entire condition, however,
could be in the past, present, or future.
-->

So,
<!--
if we use a caseless verb
as a C-verb or a CR-verb
then it will indicate a completed action.
The tense is determined by when the scenario of C will occur.
-->
if the scenario of the condition occurred in the past,
then the conditional sentence will have a past tense.
For example^[The following "Nile delta" examples are adapted from Reilly, J.\ S.\ "The acquisition of temporals and conditionals", In: Traugott, et al, (eds.) _On Conditionals_, Cambridge, 1986, p.\ 313, cited by Alfraidi, _Conditional sentences in Modern Written Arabic_ p.\ 94.]:

(@) [إِنْ مَطَرَتْ مِصْرُ عَامَ الْأَوَّلِ سَالَتْ بَطَائِحُ النِّيلِ]{.ar}  
    [If it rained in Egypt last year, the Nile delta [must have] flooded.]{.trans}

(@) [إِنْ تَمْطَرْ مِصْرُ عَامَ الْأَوَّلِ تَسِلْ بَطَائِحُ النِّيلِ]{.ar}  
    [If it [conceivably] rained in Egypt last year, the  Nile delta [should have] flooded.]{.trans}

(@) [إِذَا مَطَرَتْ مِصْرُ عَامَ الْأَوَّلِ سَالَتْ بَطَائِحُ النِّيلِ]{.ar}  
    [When it rained in Egypt last year, the Nile delta flooded.]{.trans}

The verb form [قَتَلَ]{.ar} is typically used for Cs that have a past tense.
[يَقْتُلْ]{.ar},
as in example () above, would be rare.

Similarly,
if the scenario of the condition is expected in the future,
then the conditional sentence will have a future tense.
For example:

(@) [إِنْ مَطَرَتْ مِصْرُ الْعَامَ الْمُقْبِلَ سَالَتْ بَطَائِحُ النِّيلِ]{.ar}  
    [If it [will have] rained in Egypt next year, the Nile delta [will have] flooded.]{.trans}

(@) [إِنْ تَمْطُرْ مِصْرُ الْعَامَ الْمُقْبِلَ تَسِلْ بَطَائِحُ النِّيلِ]{.ar}  
    [If it [should] rain in Egypt next year, the Nile delta [should] flood.]{.trans}

(@) [إِذَا مَطَرَتْ مِصْرُ الْعَامَ الْمُقْبِلَ سَالَتْ بَطَائِحُ النِّيلِ]{.ar}  
    [When it [will have] rained in Egypt next year, the Nile delta [will have] flooded.]{.trans}

Present:

(@) [If it is raining right now then bring the clothes in.]{.trans}


In the above examples, we have used
explicit adverbs
([this year, next year]{.trans})
to limit
the condition to the past or the future (respectively).
In the absence of such an explicit delimiter,
the tense of the C will have to be determined from context.
Consider for example the conditional sentence:

(@) [إِنْ أَحْسَنْتَ إِلَيَّ أَحْسَنْتُ إِلَيْكَ]{.ar}  

In a past context, this conditional sentence can have a past meaning:

(@) [زَعَمْتَ أَنِّي لَمْ أَجْزِكَ بَمَا صَنَعْتَ إِلَيَّ مَعْرُوفًا قُلْتُ إِنْ أَحْسَنْتَ إِلَيَّ أَحْسَنْتُ إِلَيْكَ]{.ar}  
    [You claim that I did not recompense you for your doing me a favor.
    I say that if you were good to me then I was good to you.]{.trans}  

And in a future context, the same conditional sentence can have a future meaning:

(@) [أَعِدُكَ إِنْ أَحْسَنْتَ إِلَيَّ أَحْسَنْتُ إِلَيْكَ]{.ar}  
    [I promise you: if you will be good to me, I will be good to you.]{.trans}  

<!--
(@) [إِنْ زُرْتَنِي أَمْسِ أَكْرَمْتُكَ]{.ar}  
    [If you visited me yesteday, then I honored you.]{.trans}  
-->


Or a conditional sentence may express a general truth
that holds for all times.
For example:

(@) [إِنْ مَطَرَتْ مِصْرُ سَالَتْ بَطَائِحُ النِّيلِ]{.ar}  
    [If rains in Egypt, the Nile delta floods.]{.trans}

<!--
(@) [إِنْ تَمْطُرْ مِصْرُ تَسِلْ بَطَائِحُ النِّيلِ]{.ar}  
    [If it [should] rain in Egypt, the Nile delta [should] flood.]{.trans}
-->

(@) [إِذَا مَطَرَتْ مِصْرُ سَالَتْ بَطَائِحُ النِّيلِ]{.ar}  
    [When[ever] it rains in Egypt, the Nile delta floods.]{.trans}

In addition to the differences in tense that are signified by [قَتَلَ]{.ar} vs [يَقْتُلْ]{.ar},
there can also be a difference implied in how more or less certain the condition is.
[قَتَلَ]{.ar} verb signifies more certainty than [يَقْتُلْ]{.ar}
so it is more often used for generic and general truth conditional sentences.
[يَقْتُلْ]{.ar},
on the other hand,
implies more doubt regarding whether the condition will be (or was) satisfied or not.
Examples:


Above we have discussed the difference between [قَتَلَ]{.ar} and [يَقْتُلْ]{.ar}.
As for [يَقْتُلُ]{.ar}, it indicates more certainty than [يَقْتُلْ]{.ar}.
So it can be used to indicate a habitual or on-going action
that definitely occurs when the condition is satisfied. Reusing example ():

(@) [وإن أتاه خليلٌ يومَ مسألةٍ ... **يقولُ** لا غائبٌ مالي ولا حَرِمُ]{.ar}
    [[[كتاب سيبويه]{.ar} 3/66](https://shamela.ws/book/23018/919#p1)]{.citex}  
    [And if a friend came to him on a day of asking, he **[habitually] says**, "My wealth is neither absent nor denied."]{.trans}

Or [يَقْتُلُ]{.ar} is used when the CR is meant to express an expectation or order.^[Wright 2/39D]
For example:

(@) [إِنْ أَفْرَجْتُ عَنْكُمْ تَخْرُجُونَ وَتَأْخُذُونَ بِأَيْدِيكُمْ مَا تَجِدُونَ فِي الْأَسْوَاقِ مِنْ آلَاتٍ وَأَخْشَابٍ]{.ar}
    [Wright 2/39D]{.citex}  
    [If I let you go, you [must] go out and take with your hands what you find in the markets of implements and timbers.]{.trans}  

In summary,
we would like to say that
the above the differences in the significations of the verb forms
are at best,
our attempt to extract
general trends,
and not hard and fast rules.
Many times, the verb forms are interchangeable for more or less the same meaning.
Here are some examples:


<!--
TODO: change to 3rd person for most general case. Because 1st/2nd person implies knowledge that condition did not actually take place in the past.

(@) [توقع زيد إتيان عمرو أمس.  وإِنْ أَتَيْتَهُ عَمرٌو أَكْرَمَهُ زَيٌدٌ.]{.ar}  
    [Zayd expected [#eamr]{.trn2}s coming to him yesterday.
     And if [it **was** the case that] [#eamr]{.trn2} came to him [yesterday], then ;
    [I expected your coming to me yesterday.
    And
    if you **had come** to me,
    I **would have honored** you.]{.trans}

And if, the scenario of the condition is expected in the future,
then the conditional sentence will have a future tense.
For example:


(@) [أَتوقع إتيانك إياي غدا.  وإِنْ أَتَيْتَنِي أَكْرَمْتُكَ]{.ar}  
    [I expect your coming to me tomorrow.
    And
    if you **will have come** to me,
    I **will have honored** you.]{.trans}

Similarly,
if we use a ø-case caseful verb
as a C-verb or a CR-verb
then it will indicate a potential action.
The tense is again determined by when the scenario of C will occur.

If, for example, the scenario of the condition occurred in the past,
then the conditional sentence will have a past tense.
For example:

TODO: use 3rd person as most general in order not to color from 1st and 2nd person

(@) [توقع زيد إتيان عمرو أمس وإِنْ يَأْتِه أَكْرَمَهُ]{.ar}
    [Zayd expected [#eamr]{.trn2}'s coming yesterday. And if he **would have come** to him, he **would have honored** him.]{.trans}

Having said this, the usage of ø-case C-verb with a past signification is going to be rare.
For a desired past tense, we are more likely to use [كَانَ]{.ar}
with a caseless verb in the C, thus:

(@) [ان كان قد أتاه أكرمه]{.ar}

In most cases, the ø-case; caseful verb
will have a future signification.
For example:

(@) [يتوقع زيد إتيان عمرو غدا وإِنْ يَأْتِه يُكْرِمْهُ]{.ar}
    [Zayd expects [#eamr]{.trn2}'s coming tomorrow. And if he **would come** to him, he **would honor** him.]{.trans}
-->

### The tense of a C with a static verb

The examples we gave in the previous section
showed how the tense of a C can be the past or the future.
But what if we would like the tense of a C to be the present?
This is also possible, but the C-verb in this case is usually a *static* verb.
We define a static verb,
in the context of conditional sentences to be one of the following^[from Peled, _Conditional structures in Classical Arabic_ 13]:

1. [كَانَ]{.ar}
2. An intransitive verb that has an adjectival meaning.
   For example:
   [كَثُرَ]{.ar} [to be many]{.trans},
   [صَحَّ]{.ar} [to be correct]{.trans},
   etc.
3. A _modal_ verb that takes a [أَنْ]{.ar} clause as a direct doee.
   For example:
   [شَاءَ]{.ar} [to wish, want]{.trans},
   [اسْتَطَاعَ]{.ar} [can]{.trans},
   etc.
4. A verb of perception.
   For example:
   [عَلِمَ]{.ar} [to know]{.trans},
   [ظَنَّ]{.ar} [to think]{.trans},
   [حَسِبَ]{.ar} [to consider]{.trans},
   etc.

We will treat [كَانَ]{.ar} separately in the next section.
Here we will give examples of the other kinds of static verbs in conditional sentences.

(@) [عَنِ ابْنِ مَسْعُودٍ أَنَّ النَّبِيَّ ﷺ قَالَ الرِّبَا **وَإِنْ كَثُرَ** فَإِنَّ عَاقِبَتَهُ تَصِيرُ إِلَى قُلٍّ]{.ar}
    [<https://hadithunlocked.com/ahmad:3754>]{.citex}  
    [On the authority of Ibn [#maseUd]{.trn2} that the Messenger of Allāh [ﷺ]{.aralt} said, "Interest, even if it **is much**, its outcome becomes less."]{.trans}  

(@) [فَإِن طَلَّقَهَا فَلَا جُنَاحَ عَلَیۡهِمَاۤ أَن یَتَرَاجَعَاۤ **إِن ظَنَّاۤ** أَن یُقِیمَا حُدُودَ ٱللَّهِۗ]{.quran}
    [[[سورة البقرة]{.ar} 2:230](https://quran.com/2/230)]{.citex}  
    [And if he [i.e., the latter husband] divorces her [or dies], there is no blame upon them [i.e., the woman and her former husband] for returning to each other if **they think** that they can keep [within] the limits of Allāh.]{.trans}  

For modal verbs like
[شَاءَ]{.ar} [to wish, want]{.trans},
[اسْتَطَاعَ]{.ar} [can]{.trans},
etc.,
if the [أَنْ]{.ar} clause direct doee of the modal verb is in the C
then it need not be repeated in the CR.
The CR
can then have an empty verb like [فَعَلَ]{.ar}.^[Peled, _Conditional structures in Classical Arabic_ 22]
Example:

(@) [إِنْ **أَحَبَّ** أَهْلُكِ أَنْ أَصُبَّ لَهُمْ ثَمَنَكِ صَبَّةً وَاحِدَةً فَأُعْتِقَكِ فَعَلْتُ]{.ar}
    [[[صحيح البخاري]{.ar} :2564](https://sunnah.com/bukhari:2564) cited by Peled, _Conditional structures in Classical Arabic_ 22]{.citex}  
    [If your people **like** that I pay them your price in a lump sum and so manumit you, I will do so.]{.trans}  

<https://sunnah.com/bukhari:554>

Or the
[أَنْ]{.ar} clause direct doee of the modal verb can be omitted from the C
when its meaning is given in the CR.
For example:

(@) [إِنْ **شِئْتَ** دَفَعْتُهَا إِلَيْكَ]{.ar}
    [[[صحيح البخاري]{.ar} :3828](https://sunnah.com/bukhari:3828) cited by Peled, _Conditional structures in Classical Arabic_ 22]{.citex}  
    [If you **wish**, I will give her to you]{.trans}  


## [كَانَ]{.ar} as the C-verb

The use of [كَانَ]{.ar} as the C-verb deserves special treatment.
We will discuss several of its uses in the following subsections:

### Using [كَانَ]{.ar} to explicitly restrict the tense of a verbal sentence in a C

In section\ (ref) above,
we learned that the 
C-intro allows the tense of the C to be open to all times.
So for example,
if we say:

(@) [إِنْ ذَهَبْتَ ذَهَبْتُ مَعَكَ]{.ar}  
    [If you go, I go with you.]{.trans}  

then its tense can be the past, future, or be valid for all times.

But what if we wish to explicitly restrict the tense of the C to the present or the past?
We can do this by transforming the sentence in the C
to be the ;xabar of [كَانَ]{.ar}.
[كَانَ]{.ar} will then be the C-verb.
And the tense of the C will be the tense of the info or [كَانَ]{.ar}.

For example, say that we would like to
restrict the tense of the C in example () above to the present.
So we would like to say
[if you are going, I will go with you]{.trans}.
We first start with the sentence
[you are going]{.trans}.
This is expressed in Arabic using the u-case caseful verb
[تَذْهَبُ]{.ar}.
In order to retain the tense of the sentence in a C,
we begin the sentence with [كَانَ]{.ar} and make it the C-verb, thus:

(@) [إِنْ كُنْتَ تَذْهَبُ أَذْهَبُ مَعَكَ]{.ar}  
    [If **you** are going, I will go with you.]{.trans}  

Note that [تَذْهَبُ]{.ar} is in the u-case because it is not the C-verb.

Similarly,
let's say we wish to say
[If you saw him, then why didn't you invite him?]{.trans}
We first start with the sentence of the C:
[you saw him]{.trans}.
This is expressed in Arabic with
[رَأَيْتَهُ]{.ar}.
Now we add [كَانَ]{.ar} before it and, optionally, a [قَدْ]{.ar} as well.
So we say:

(@) [إِنْ كُنْتَ قَدْ رَأَيْتَهُ فَمَا لَكَ لَمْ تَدْعُهُ]{.ar}  
    [If you **saw** him, then why didn't you invite him?]{.trans}

By the way, we already know, that in context, the verb
[رَأَيْتَ]{.ar} can be sufficient for the past tense as well:

(@) [إِنْ رَأَيْتَهُ فَمَا لَكَ لَمْ تَدْعُهُ]{.ar}  
    [If you saw him, why didn't you invite him?]{.trans}

Now, what if the tense that we would like to specify in the C
already begins with [كَانَ]{.ar}?
For example, what if we would like to say
[If you **were** going, why didn't you take him with you?]{.trans}
The sentence in the C is
[you were going]{.trans}.
This is expressed in Arabic as
[كُنْتَ تَذْهَبُ]{.ar}.
When we put this sentence in a C
then we don't add another [كَانَ]{.ar}.
So the full conditional sentence is:

(@) [إِنْ كُنْتَ تَذْهَبُ فَمَا لَكَ لَمْ تَذْهَبْ بِهِ]{.ar}  
    [If you were going, why didn't you take him with you?]{.trans}

So you can see from examples () and () above that
[إِنْ كُنْتَ تَذْهَبُ]{.ar}
can mean both
[if you are going]{.trans}
and
[if you were going]{.trans}.
But both ways, the tense of the C is explicitly restricted.

Here are some more examples of Cs with [كَانَ]{.ar}:

(@) [قَالَ فَمَا لِي لَا أَعْتَدُّ بِهَا وَإِنْ كُنْتُ قَدْ عَجَزْتُ وَاسْتَحْمَقْتُ]{.ar}
    [[[مسند أحمد]{.ar} :304](https://sunnah.com/ahmad:304)]{.citex}  

(@) [قَالَ لَهَا وَإِنْ **كَانَ لَمْ يُؤْذَنْ** لِي فِي ثَقِيفٍ يَا خُوَيْلَةُ]{.ar}
    [[[تاريخ الطبري]{.ar} 3/85](https://shamela.ws/book/9783/1359) cited by Peled, _Conditional structures in Classical Arabic_ 33]{.citex}  
    [He ([ﷺ]{.aralt}) said to her, "And if [#vaqIf]{.trn2} **has not been permitted** for me, O [#xuwaylah]{.trn2}?"]{.trans}  

(@) [إِنْ **كَانَ لَا يَضُرُّكِ وَلَا يُضَيِّقُ عَلَيْكِ** فَإِنِّي أُحِبُّ أَنْ أُدْفَنَ مَعَ صَاحِبَيَّ]{.ar}
    [<https://hadithunlocked.com/hakim:4519>]{.citex}  
    [If it **does not harm you or make it difficult for you** then I would like that I be buried with my two companions.]{.trans}  

(@) [مَنْ قَالَ أَسْتَغْفِرُ اللَّهَ الَّذِي لاَ إِلَهَ إِلاَّ هُوَ الْحَىُّ الْقَيُّومُ وَأَتُوبُ إِلَيْهِ غُفِرَ لَهُ وَإِنْ **كَانَ فَرَّ** مِنَ الزَّحْفِ]{.ar}
    [[[سنن أبي داود]{.ar} :1517](https://sunnah.com/abudawud:1517)]{.citex}  
    [Whoever says, "I seek forgiveness from Allāh, the one whom there is no deity except He, the Ever-Living, the Self-Sustaining, and I turn to Him in repentance," he is forgiven even if he **had fled** from battle.]{.trans}

(@) [إِنْ كُنْتَ تُرِيدُ أَنْ تُصِيبَ السُّنَّةَ الْيَوْمَ فَاقْصُرِ الْخُطْبَةَ وَعَجِّلِ الْوُقُوفَ]{.ar}
    [bukhari:1663]{.citex}  

The ø-case caseful verb [يَكُنْ]{.ar} can also be used in this manner.
Consistent with what we learned in section\ (ref) above,
it may indicate potentiality or possibility.
For example:

(@) [فَإِن **يكن قد علا رَأْسِي** وَغَيره ... صرف الزَّمَان وتغيير من الشّعْر]{.ar}
    [[[جمهرة الأمثال للعسكري]{.ar} 1/263](https://shamela.ws/book/6897/261)]{.citex}  
    [So if **it be that my head has become dignified** and the elapsing of time and a changing of hair has changed it ...]{.trans}

(@) [فإن تكن الدنيا بلبنى تغيرت فللدهر والدنيا بطون وأظهر]{.ar}
    [cited by gatge dur struktur 175]{.citex}

### Using [كَانَ]{.ar} with a nounal sentence in a C

We have learned so far
that both the C and the CR require verbal sentences.

But what do we do if we would like the C to be a nounal sentence?
For example,
if we would like to say:
[If you are sick, we shall go to Cairo]{.trans},
then the condition clause
[you are sick]{.trans}
can be  translated as
[أَنْتَ مَرِيضٌ]{.ar}.

In order to form C
from this nounal sentence,
we again transform it to a [كَانَ]{.ar} sentence.
So
[أَنْتَ مَرِيضٌ]{.ar}
becomes
[كُنْتَ مَرِيضًا]{.ar}.
The verb [كَانَ]{.ar} becomes the C-verb
and we can now
form our conditional sentence:

(@) [إِنْ كُنْتَ مَرِيضًا سَافَرْنَا إِلَى الْقَاهِرَةِ]{.ar}
    [Peled, _Conditional structures in Classical Arabic_ 13]{.citex}  
    [If you are sick, we shall go to Cairo.]{.trans}

Here are some more examples:

(@) [وَإِن **كُنتُمۡ** فِی رَیۡبࣲ مِّمَّا نَزَّلۡنَا عَلَىٰ عَبۡدِنَا فَأۡتُوا۟ بِسُورَةࣲ مِّن مِّثۡلِهِۦ وَٱدۡعُوا۟ شُهَدَاۤءَكُم مِّن دُونِ ٱللَّهِ إِن كُنتُمۡ صَـٰدِقِینَ]{.quran}
    [[[سورة البقرة]{.ar} 2:23](https://quran.com/2/23)]{.citex}  
    [And if you **are** in doubt about what We have sent down [i.e., the Qur’ān] upon Our Servant [i.e., Prophet Muḥammad (ﷺ)], then produce a sūrah the like thereof and call upon your witnesses [i.e., supporters] other than Allāh, if you should be truthful.]{.trans}

(@) [إِذَا كَانَ الْمَاءُ قُلَّتَيْنِ لَمْ يَحْمِلِ الْخَبَثَ]{.ar}
    [[[سنن النسائي]{.ar} :52](https://sunnah.com/nasai:52)]{.citex}  
    [When the water is two qullahs then it will not carry filth.]{.trans}  

(@) [فَإِنْ كُنْتُمْ أَكْفَاءَ قَاتَلْنَاكُمْ]{.ar}
    [[[مغازي الواقدي]{.ar} 1/68](https://shamela.ws/book/23680/109)]{.citex}  
    [If you are a match for us we will fight you.]{.trans}

Consistent with what we stated in section\ (ref) above,
if the C's sentence already has [كَانَ]{.ar}
to indicate the past tense, then another [كَانَ]{.ar} is not added.
For example:

<!--
(@) [كُنْتُ آتِي النَّبِيَّ صَلَّى اللَّهُ عَلَيْهِ وَسَلَّمَ فَأَسْتَأْذِنُ فَإِنَّ **كَانَ** فِي صَلَاةٍ سَبَّحَ وَإِنْ **كَانَ** فِي غَيْرِ صَلَاةٍ أَذِنَ لِي]{.ar}
    [[[مسند أحمد]{.ar} :809](https://sunnah.com/ahmad:809)]{.citex}  
    [I used to come to the Prophet [ﷺ]{.aralt} and ask permission to enter. If he **was** in prayer he would say [subHana -llAh]{.trn} and if he **was** not in prayer, he would permit me.]{.trans}  
-->

(@) [كَانَ يَنَامُ أَوَّلَ اللَّيْلِ ثُمَّ يَقُومُ فَإِذَا **كَانَ** مِنَ السَّحَرِ أَوْتَرَ ثُمَّ أَتَى فِرَاشَهُ فَإِذَا **كَانَ** لَهُ حَاجَةٌ أَلَمَّ بِأَهْلِهِ فَإِذَا سَمِعَ الأَذَانَ وَثَبَ فَإِنْ **كَانَ** جُنُبًا أَفَاضَ عَلَيْهِ مِنَ الْمَاءِ وَإِلاَّ تَوَضَّأَ ثُمَّ خَرَجَ إِلَى الصَّلاَةِ]{.ar}
    [[[سنن النسائي]{.ar} :1680](https://sunnah.com/nasai:1680)]{.citex}  
    [He would sleep in the first part of the night. Then he would stand (for prayer). Then when it was the last part of the night, he would pray the witr. Then he would come to his bed. Then if he had a need he would go to his wife. Then when he heard the [apAn]{.trn2}, he would get up. If we was in a state of sexual impurity he poured water over himself. And if not he would perform [wudUE]{.trn2}. Then he would go out to the prayer.]{.trans}  


[كَانَ]{.ar} can also, in context, be used for a nounal sentence with a future significance.
For example:

(@) [إِنْ كَانَ لَهُ عَمَلٌ صَالِحٌ أُخِذَ مِنْهُ بِقَدْرِ مَظْلَمَتِهِ]{.ar}
    [[[صحيح البخاري]{.ar} :2449](https://sunnah.com/bukhari:2449)]{.citex}  
    [If he will have good deeds, they will be taken from him according to his oppression.]{.trans}  

The ø-case caseful verb [يَكُنْ]{.ar} can again be used.
For example:

(@) [إِنْ **يَكُ** هَذَا مِنْ عِنْدِ اللَّهِ يُمْضِهِ]{.ar}
    [[[صحيح البخاري]{.ar} :3895](https://sunnah.com/bukhari:3895) cited by Peled, _Conditional structures in Classical Arabic_ 21]{.citex}  
    [If this **be** from Allāh, He will make it happen.]{.trans}

(@) [فَإِنْ **يَكُ** صَوَابًا فَمِنَ اللَّهِ وَإِنْ **يَكُنْ** خَطَأً فَمِنِّي وَمِنَ الشَّيْطَانِ وَاللَّهُ وَرَسُولُهُ بَرِيئَانِ]{.ar}
    [[[سنن أبي داود]{.ar} :2116](https://sunnah.com/abudawud:2116)]{.citex}  
    [If this **be** correct then [it is] from Allāh, and if it **be** erroneous then [it is] from me and from Satan. And Allāh and His Messenger are free [from it].]{.trans}  

### Using [كَانَ]{.ar} and a doer participle to indicate intention

Remember from section\ (ref)
that the doer participle can be used to indicate an intention to do a future action.
For example:

إِنْ كُنْتَ فَاعِلاً فَوَاحِدَةً

bukhari:1207

This use of the doer participle can carry over to its use in a C.
Peled 75--76


## A noun before the C-verb

Rework: https://shamela.ws/book/23018/965,
https://shamela.ws/book/17116/2004

peled p. 27 examples 36 and 38; pp. 19--20

Wright 2/39--40A

The basic principle for a C-core is that it should be a verbal sentence.
(We include [كَانَ]{.ar} sentences in verbal sentences, loosely speaking.)
And the verb which begins the C-core is classified as the C-verb.

<!--
We had earlier stated that the C requires a C-verb in its C-core.
Based on the preponderance of data in attested usage,
the grammarians extracted a basic principle that the C-core
containing this C-verb should be a verbal sentence.
-->
In all the examples we have seen so far,
this has been the case.
<!--
(We include [كَانَ]{.ar} sentences in verbal sentences, loosely speaking.)
-->
So, in the example:
[إِنْ أَتَيْتَنِي أَكْرَمْتُكَ]{.ar},
the C-core is the verbal sentence
[أَتَيْتَنِي]{.ar},
and it is begun with the C-verb [أَتَى]{.ar}.

Occassionally,
however,
the C-core is found to begin with a noun.
<!--
This noun is usually a detached pronoun, or an indefinite noun.
-->
Here are some examples:

(@) [إِنْ **أَنْتَ** أَكْرَمْتَ اللَّئِيمَ تَمَرَّدَا]{.ar}
    [abdelghani 96]{.citex}  
    [If you honor an ignoble [person], he rebels]{.trans}  
    (An extra alif is suffixed to [تَمَرَّدَ]{.ar} for rhyme.)

(@) [إذا أنت لم تنفع فضرّ]{.ar}
    [abdelghani 97]{.citex}  

<!--
Because such structures are relatively rare compared to
C-cores that begin with a verbal sentence,
the grammarians attempted to keep the basic principle consistent.
Which is that the C-core should be a verbal sentence.
-->
In order to align outlier examples like () and () above with the basic principle
that the C-core should be a verbal sentence,
the grammarians virtually model the C-core as a verbal sentence with a deleted verb.
So the virtual model of the C for example () above would be:

(@) [إِنْ [أَكْرَمْتَ اللَّئِيم]]{.ar}

The C-core in the virtual model is
[[أَكْرَمْتَ اللَّئِيم]]{.ar}
which is a verbal sentence.
In order to transform this virtual model into the surface speech
[إِنْ أَنْتَ أَكْرَمْتَ اللَّئِيم]{.ar},
the following theoretical steps are taken:

  i. The 
     virtually modeled verbal sentence
     [[أَكْرَمْتَ اللَّئِيم]]{.ar}
     is deleted, except for the doer of the C-verb.
 ii. The C-verb's doer is left in place.
     In this case, it is the
     the attached pronoun [تَ]{.ar}.
     But because the 
     an attached pronoun cannot occur by itself,
     it is converted to the detached pronoun
     [أَنْتَ]{.ar}.
iii. An explanatory verbal sentence
     [أَكْرَمْتَ اللَّئِيم]{.ar}
     is added after the doer [أَنْتَ]{.ar} in order to give the meaning of
     virtually modeled verbal sentence.
     In this (and most) cases, the explanatory verbal sentence matches the virtually modeled original C-core.
     The verb in the explanatory verbal sentence is now the new C-verb.


The benefit of this virtual modeling,
and of mandating a verbal sentence as the C-core,
is that it prevents arbitrary nounal sentences,
that do not have a verb as the C-verb,
from being the C-core.
<!--
This is because such C-cores are not found in attested usage.
-->
So for example, we cannot say:

(@) ✗ [إِنْ أَنْتَ كَرِيمٌ ...]{.ar}  
    [If you are honorable ...]{.trans}

Let's take a look at another example:

(@) [ فإن أنت لم ينفعك علمك فانتسب ... لعلّك تهديك القرون الأوائل]{.ar}  
    [cited by Abdel-Ghani, _Conditional sentences within the Arab grammatical tradition_ 101; [[شرح التسهيل لابن مالك]{.ar} 2/140](https://shamela.ws/book/13257/536)]{.citex}  
    [If your knowledge does not benefit you then affiliate yourself [to your predecessors]. Perhaps the earlier generations can guide you.]{.trans}

In example () above,
the additional complication is that the doer [أَنْتَ]{.ar}
that begins the C-core, does not match the doer
[عِلْمُكَ]{.ar}
of the explanatory verbal sentence
[لم ينفعك علمك]{.ar}.^[Abdel-Ghani, _Conditional sentences within the Arab grammatical tradition_ 101--102]
But we can still mold this to fit in with out basic principle and the transformational steps that we gave above. Here's how:

The virtual model for the C can be virtually modeled as:

(@) [فإن [لم تنتفع بعلمك]]{.ar}

The virtually modeled verbal sentence
[[لم تنتفع بعلمك]]{.ar}
is deleted except for the C-verb [تنتفع]{.ar}'s doer, the latent pronoun [[أَنْتَ]]{.ar}.
This latent doer pronoun
is transformed for surface speech into the detached pronoun [أَنْتَ]{.ar}.
An explanatory verbal sentence
[لم ينفعك علمك]{.ar}
is added after the doer
[أَنْتَ]{.ar}
to give the meaning of
a
virtually modeled verbal sentence.
In this case, the explanatory verbal sentence does not exactly match the virtually modeled original C-core in wording,
but has a similar meaning.^[[[التذييل والتكميل لأبي حيان]{.ar} 6/313](https://shamela.ws/book/17116/2004)]

Here are some more examples of this kind of conditional sentence:

TODO: get examples from Qurʾān and from peled.

(@) [فإنْ يَكُ عامِرٌ قد قالَ جَهلاً فإنَّ مَظِنَّةَ الجَهْلِ الشّبابُ]
    [cited by Gätje, "Zur Struktur gestörter Konditionalgefüge im Arabischen" 174]{.citex}  

From the above examples, we can note the following points:

* The C-verb is always [قَتَلَ]{.ar} (or [لَمْ يَقْتُلْ]{.ar} which is equivalent).
* The original doer before the C-verb is usually either a pronoun, or a generic or indefinite noun.
  There very few cases, where it is a definite specific noun.

## The [لَوْ]{.ar} conditional sentence

Versteegh, "Two conceptions of irreality"

## [وَإِنْ]{.ar} and [وَلَوْ]{.ar} used for [even if]{.trans}

Wright 2/40A

## The deleted C or CR

Wright 2/17 Rem b

## deleted CR-verb

bukhari:1116

صَلِّ قَائِمًا، فَإِنْ لَمْ تَسْتَطِعْ فَقَاعِدًا

## The conditional sentence combined with an oath

Wright 2/39B

[[شرح ابن يعيش على المفصل]{.ar} 5/142](https://shamela.ws/book/13301/2080#p1)

[النحو الوافي]{.ar} 4/482

## conjunction in the C or the CR

Wright 2/40CD

## Consecutive conditions

[النحو الوافي]{.ar} 4/489

also condition followed by question

## Further reading

1. CONDITIONAL SENTENCES WITHIN THE ARAB GRAMMATICAL TRADITION by AHMED ABDEL-AZIM ABDEL-GHANI, Ph.D. thesis, The University of Leeds February 1981.
2. CONDITIONAL STRUCTURE IN CLASSICAL ARABIC: A GENERAL DESCRIPTIVE STUDY, SALMAN AL-SAAD PhD The University of London 2010.
3. [الدلالة الزمنبة للجملة الشرطية في النحو العربي لنور الدين قفي]{.ar}
4. [الجملة الشرطية عند النحاة العرب لأبي أوس إبراهيم الشمسان]{.ar}
5. [آراء وأفكار حول الجملة الشرطية في العربية عبد العليم بوفاتح]{.ar}
6. [التأويل الزمني للجملة الشرطية في اللغة العربية لعارف الدين]{.ar}
7. TRK Alfraidi, Conditional sentences in Modern Written Arabic
8. Helmut Gätje, Zur Struktur gestörter Konditionalgefüge im Arabischen

---

لَئِنْ أَعْطَيْتَنِيهِ لاَ يُخْلَصُ إِلَيْهِمْ أَبَدًا
3110

لَوْ أَنَّ لَكَ مَا فِي الأَرْضِ مِنْ شَىْءٍ كُنْتَ تَفْتَدِي بِهِ
3334

إِنْ كُنْتَ كَاذِبًا فَصَيَّرَكَ اللَّهُ إِلَى مَا كُنْتَ
3464

فَإِذَا سَمِعْتُمْ بِهِ بِأَرْضٍ فَلاَ تَقْدَمُوا عَلَيْهِ، وَإِذَا وَقَعَ بِأَرْضٍ وَأَنْتُمْ بِهَا فَلاَ تَخْرُجُوا فِرَارًا مِنْهُ
3473

إِذَا لَمْ تَسْتَحِي فَافْعَلْ مَا شِئْتَ
3482

إِذَا اخْتَلَفْتُمْ أَنْتُمْ وَزَيْدُ بْنُ ثَابِتٍ فِي شَىْءٍ مِنَ الْقُرْآنِ، فَاكْتُبُوهُ بِلِسَانِ قُرَيْشٍ
3506

إِنْ كَتَمْتَ عَلَىَّ أَخْبَرْتُكَ
3522

فَإِنْ طَالَتْ بِكَ حَيَاةٌ لَتَرَيَنَّ الظَّعِينَةَ تَرْتَحِلُ مِنَ الْحِيرَةِ، حَتَّى تَطُوفَ بِالْكَعْبَةِ
وَلَئِنْ طَالَتْ بِكَ حَيَاةٌ لَتُفْتَحَنَّ كُنُوزُ كِسْرَى
3595

لَوْ سَأَلْتَنِي هَذِهِ الْقِطْعَةَ مَا أَعْطَيْتُكَهَا، وَلَنْ تَعْدُوَ أَمْرَ اللَّهِ فِيكَ، وَلَئِنْ أَدْبَرْتَ لَيَعْقِرَنَّكَ اللَّهُ،
3620

إِنْ كُنْتَ إِنَّمَا اشْتَرَيْتَنِي لِنَفْسِكَ فَأَمْسِكْنِي، وَإِنْ كُنْتَ إِنَّمَا اشْتَرَيْتَنِي لِلَّهِ فَدَعْنِي وَعَمَلَ اللَّهِ
3755

قَالَ إِنْ أَعْطَيْتَنِي عَهْدًا وَمِيثَاقًا لَتُرْشِدَنَّنِي فَعَلْتُ
3861

لَوْلاَ أَنَّكَ مَعَ أَبِي صَفْوَانَ مَا رَجَعْتَ إِلَى أَهْلِكَ سَالِمًا. فَقَالَ لَهُ سَعْدٌ وَرَفَعَ صَوْتَهُ عَلَيْهِ أَمَا وَاللَّهِ لَئِنْ مَنَعْتَنِي هَذَا لأَمْنَعَنَّكَ مَا هُوَ أَشَدُّ عَلَيْكَ مِنْهُ
3950

إِنْ شِئْتَ أَنْكَحْتُكَ حَفْصَةَ بِنْتَ عُمَرَ
4005

إِنْ رَأَيْتُمُونَا ظَهَرْنَا عَلَيْهِمْ فَلاَ تَبْرَحُوا
وَإِنْ رَأَيْتُمُوهُمْ ظَهَرُوا عَلَيْنَا فَلاَ تُعِينُونَا
4043

إِنْ قَتَلْتَ حَمْزَةَ بِعَمِّي فَأَنْتَ حُرٌّ
4072

فَقَالُوا لَكُمُ الْعَهْدُ وَالْمِيثَاقُ إِنْ نَزَلْتُمْ إِلَيْنَا أَنْ لاَ نَقْتُلَ مِنْكُمْ رَجُلاً.
4086

وَإِنْ كُنْتَ تُرِيدُ الْمَالَ فَسَلْ مِنْهُ مَا شِئْتَ
4372

فَإِذَا رَأَيْتَ الَّذِينَ يَتَّبِعُونَ مَا تَشَابَهَ مِنْهُ، فَأُولَئِكَ الَّذِينَ سَمَّى اللَّهُ، فَاحْذَرُوهُمْ
4547

إِنْ تَسْتَغْفِرْ لَهُمْ سَبْعِينَ مَرَّةً فَلَنْ يَغْفِرَ اللَّهُ لَهُمْ

إِنْ كُنْتِ بَرِيئَةً فَسَيُبَرِّئُكِ اللَّهُ، وَإِنْ كُنْتِ أَلْمَمْتِ بِذَنْبٍ فَاسْتَغْفِرِي اللَّهَ وَتُوبِي إِلَيْهِ
4690

فَإِنْ لَمْ تَكُونُوا بَاكِينَ فَلاَ تَدْخُلُوا عَلَيْهِمْ
4702

وَإِنْ تَسْأَلِ الْجَارِيَةَ تَصْدُقْكَ
4750

فَإِنْ لَمْ تَكُنْ تَرَاهُ فَإِنَّهُ يَرَاكَ
4777

 فَإِنْ كَانَ لِي عِلْمٌ خَبَّرْتُكَ بِهِ
 4913

إِنْ لَمْ يَكُنْ لَكَ بِهَا حَاجَةٌ فَزَوِّجْنِيهَا
إِنْ لَبِسْتَهُ لَمْ يَكُنْ عَلَيْهَا مِنْهُ شَىْءٌ وَإِنْ لَبِسَتْهُ لَمْ يَكُنْ عَلَيْكَ شَىْءٌ
5030

فَإِذَا اخْتَلَفْتُمْ فَقُومُوا عَنْهُ
5060

إِذَا أَتَى أَحَدَكُمْ خَادِمُهُ بِطَعَامِهِ، فَإِنْ لَمْ يُجْلِسْهُ مَعَهُ فَلْيُنَاوِلْهُ أُكْلَةً أَوْ أُكْلَتَيْنِ، أَوْ لُقْمَةً أَوْ لُقْمَتَيْنِ،
5460

وَإِنْ وَجَدْتَ مَعَ كَلْبِكَ أَوْ كِلاَبِكَ كَلْبًا غَيْرَهُ فَخَشِيتَ أَنْ يَكُونَ أَخَذَهُ مَعَهُ، وَقَدْ قَتَلَهُ، فَلاَ تَأْكُلْ، فَإِنَّمَا ذَكَرْتَ اسْمَ اللَّهِ عَلَى كَلْبِكَ وَلَمْ تَذْكُرْهُ عَلَى غَيْرِهِ
5475

وَإِنْ خَالَطَهَا كِلاَبٌ مِنْ غَيْرِهَا فَلاَ تَأْكُلْ
5483

فَإِنْ وَجَدْتُمْ غَيْرَ آنِيَتِهِمْ، فَلاَ تَأْكُلُوا فِيهَا، وَإِنْ لَمْ تَجِدُوا فَاغْسِلُوهَا ثُمَّ كُلُوا فِيهَا
5488

فَإِذَا رَأَيْتُمْ مِنْهَا شَيْئًا فَصَلُّوا وَادْعُوا اللَّهَ حَتَّى يَكْشِفَهَا
5785

إِنْ كُنْتَ تَسْمَعُ صَوْتِي لَمَّا جِئْتَ
6140

فَإِنْ مُتَّ مُتَّ عَلَى الْفِطْرَةِ
6313

إِنْ أُوتِيتَهَا عَنْ مَسْأَلَةٍ وُكِلْتَ إِلَيْهَا، وَإِنْ أُوتِيتَهَا مِنْ غَيْرِ مَسْأَلَةٍ أُعِنْتَ عَلَيْهَا
6622

اللَّهُمَّ إِنْ كُنْتَ تَعْلَمُ فِيَّ خَيْرًا فَأَرِنِي رُؤْيَا
7028

إِنْ لَمْ تَجِدِينِي فَأْتِي أَبَا بَكْرٍ
7220
